content.headline: Soffritto cooked for 8 minutes and soffritto cooked for 2 minutes are not the same ingredient — one is the foundation of the dish, the other is just raw vegetables that have been briefly warmed.
points: Classic ratio: 2 parts onion to 1 part carrot to 1 part celery, cut to a small, even quarter-inch dice
Medium-low heat and patience — you want translucency, not browning; any darkening means the heat is too high
At 8–10 minutes the kitchen should smell caramel-sweet and savory — that smell is the signal it's ready
Garlic goes in last, after the base is soft, with 1–2 minutes remaining — added early, it burns and turns bitter
content.tell_me_more: The transformation during soffritto cooking is mostly about cell walls. Raw onion, carrot, and celery have rigid cells that trap flavor compounds inside. Slow heat in fat breaks those walls down, releasing sugars that caramelize gently and volatile aromatics that dissolve into the oil. Because fat carries flavor molecules far better than water, those released compounds get distributed through the whole dish in a way that water-based cooking can't replicate. A broth with raw aromatics tastes vegetable-forward; one built on a proper soffritto tastes complex in a way that's hard to identify as any single ingredient.

missions.title: Soffritto in the Pan
missions.description: Make a full soffritto with half an onion, one small carrot, and one celery stalk — dice everything evenly, then cook on medium-low until completely soft and fragrant.
missions.why_it_matters: The smell test is the real lesson — you can't learn it from a photo or a timer, only by cooking it yourself and noticing the moment it smells right.
missions.prompt: Show the soffritto in the pan at the end of cooking. The onion should look almost translucent or very pale golden — not white and raw-looking, but not brown either. The mixture should look soft and slightly glistening with oil.
reflection_choices: Onion turned translucent, smell went sweet and savory, no browning — cooked for the full time
Looked right but I'm not sure I gave it enough time — maybe 5–6 minutes total
Some browning on the bottom of the pan — heat was too high
Garlic went in too early and got a little dark before the onion was soft

missions.title: The Smell Timeline
missions.description: Reflect on how the smell of the soffritto changed over the course of cooking.
missions.why_it_matters: Learning to smell in stages trains you to recognize when a dish is progressing correctly without constantly tasting or checking.
missions.prompt: What did the soffritto smell like at 2–3 minutes into cooking versus at 8–10 minutes? Was there a specific moment when the smell changed from sharp or raw to something sweeter and more savory? Describe what that shift was like.
